coredata.dc:title: Microwave assisted synthesis of doped carbon dots and their application as green and simple turn off–on fluorescent sensor for mercury (II) and iodide in environmental samples
coredata.dc:creator: Tabaraki, Reza
Sadeghinejad, Negar
coredata.dc:description: A novel, green, facile and dual turn-off/on sensor for detection of Hg2+ and I- was developed based on carbon dots. Carbon dots were synthesized from citric acid, urea, and thiourea by microwave-assisted method. The size of the carbon dots (CDs) was about 10 nm and the synthesized CDs showed a strong emission at 523 nm upon excitation at 416 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield was 19.2%. Mercury (II) quenched the fluorescence of carbon dots. This turn off sensor had linear response for Hg2+ over a concentration range from 0.1 to 20 µM with detection limit as low as 62 nM. The carbon dots/Hg2+ system was also used as a turn on sensor for detection of iodide. Linear concentration range for I- was 0.1–10 µM with detection limit as low as 72 nM. The proposed method showed good sensitivity and selectivity with respect to interference ions. Finally, this system was successfully used for the detection of Hg2+ and I- in tap, river and mineral waters and fish samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Mercury
Iodide
Sensor
Microwave

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Hg2+ and I- in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of the detection process for Hg (II) and I-.
(A) UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of the CDs in aqueous solutions λex = 416 nm and λem = 523 nm. (B) FL spectra at different excitation wavelength.
(A) The typical TEM image of the CDs. (B) FTIR spectrum of the CDs.
(A) Influence of time (B) influence of pH on the relative florescence intensity in the presence of 20 × 10−6 M Hg 2+.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs in aqueous solution upon addition of various concentrations of Hg2+ (from top to bottom: 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 10, 15 and 20 μM), excitation at 416 nm. (B) Stern-Volmer plots of the CDs fluorescence-intensity quenching by Hg2+.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the carbon dots /Hg2+ complexes in aqueous solution upon addition of various concentrations of I- (from bottom to top: 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 10 μM), excitation at 416 nm. (B) Stern-Volmer plots of the carbon dots /Hg2+ complexes fluorescence-intensity enhanced by I-.
Comparison of the proposed fluorescence sensor with the some previously reported.

row.entry: CdSe quantum dots surface-modified with triethanolamine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The effect of coexisting material inclusive some amino acid, the putative transition and noble metal ions.

row.entry: Zn2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Si3+, Mn2+, Ba2+,Ti3+,Na+, Si3+, sucrose, Aspartic acid, Phenyl alanine, Aspargine, Alanine
Br-, L-Arginine, Glycine, Lysine, Proline, Serine, Methionine, Tyrosine, Leucine
F-, NO2 -, Ni2+, Mg2+, SCN-, Cl-,S2-,CH3COO-, Cu2+, glucose, urea
I-, Glutamic acid, Glutamine, Cysteine, Histidine

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Initial concentration of Hg2+ was 20 µM.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Hg2+ and iodide by the suggested fluorescence sensor in the different real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of three replicate measurements ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Microwave assisted synthesis of doped carbon dots and their application as green and simple turn off–on fluorescent sensor for mercury (II) and iodide in environmental samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Ilam University, Ilam, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel, green, facile and dual turn-off/on sensor for detection of Hg2+ and I- was developed based on carbon dots. Carbon dots were synthesized from citric acid, urea, and thiourea by microwave-assisted method. The size of the carbon dots (CDs) was about 10 nm and the synthesized CDs showed a strong emission at 523 nm upon excitation at 416 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield was 19.2%. Mercury (II) quenched the fluorescence of carbon dots. This turn off sensor had linear response for Hg2+ over a concentration range from 0.1 to 20 µM with detection limit as low as 62 nM. The carbon dots/Hg2+ system was also used as a turn on sensor for detection of iodide. Linear concentration range for I- was 0.1–10 µM with detection limit as low as 72 nM. The proposed method showed good sensitivity and selectivity with respect to interference ions. Finally, this system was successfully used for the detection of Hg2+ and I- in tap, river and mineral waters and fish samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury is one of the hazardous pollutants that found throughout the environment. Its mobile nature allows it to diffuse through the air, soils and ultimately into water systems and food chain. It is confirmed that Hg2+ can lead to DNA and central nervous system damage because it can easily pass through skin and respiratory (Bera et al., 2014). Iodide is an essential trace element and biological anion that is vital for normal growth and development. It is known because of its essential and important role for thyroid function. In fact, without iodide, thyroid hormones could not even be synthesized. Thus, dietary iodide deficiency can lead to several different problems, including poor thyroid function, goiter, neurological damage and paralysis. However, an excess of iodine can also lead to thyroid disorders, especially in infants. Hence, it is very important to approach the method to facile, rapid, sensitive and selective detection of mercury and iodide.

Up to now, several methods have been developed for the determination of trace mercury and iodide. cold-vapor atomic absorption spectroscopy, inductively-coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (Grazyna and Jerzy, 2011), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (Hsu et al., 2013), spectrophotometry (Rofouei et al., 2012), cyclic voltammetry with electrochemical liquid-phase microextraction (Zhu et al., 2010), flow injection spectrophotometric analysis (Kamavisdar and Patel, 2010) have been developed for detection of I-. These methods require expensive and sophisticated instrumentation and/or complicated sample preparation processes.

In recent years, detection of ions by fluorescent sensors has earned enormous attention because of their advantages over other techniques, such as ease detection, simple handling, high sensitivity and selectivity. New member of the carbon family is carbon dots (CDs) that is an important class of fluorescent sensors (Farka et al., 2017; Goryacheva et al., 2017; Sharma et al., 2017; Tuerhong et al., 2017; Walekar et al., 2017; Zu et al., 2017). Owing to its proper properties such as high chemical stability, low toxicity, good photo-stability, and excellent biocompatibility were used as fluorescent sensors for detection of ions (Sachdev and Gopinath, 2015; Sun et al., 2016). Different methods for CDs preparation such as arc discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, and microwave methods were established. Among these methods microwave-assisted techniques can prepare CDs with fluorescence efficiency and high selectivity in short time.

In this study, new CDs was synthesized by microwave-assisted method and used for mercury (II) and iodide detection in aqueous solution. The assay principle is shown in Fig. 1. Fluorescence of CDs was quenched after addition of Hg2+. Because of higher affinity between Hg2+ and iodide, after addition iodide to Hg2+-CDs system, Hg2+ ions released from surface of CDs and fluorescence of CDs were recovered. This process was successfully employed for determination of Hg2+ and iodide in real water and fish samples. Selectivity of synthesized CDs for Hg2+ and iodide was also studied.

All chemicals were analytical grade and obtained from Merck (Germany). Britton–Robinson buffers (0.04 M) were used in the range pH = 2–10. Stock solutions (10 mM) of Hg2+ and I- ions were prepared from HgNO3 and KI in ultrapure water. By diluting standard stock solutions, different concentrations of Hg2+ and I- ions were obtained. Stock solutions (10 mM) of metal ions (Na+, K+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Ti3+, Bi3+, Ba2+, Si4+) were prepared with ultrapure water from their salts.

Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) study was conducted with a Vertex 70 FT-IR. The UV–vis absorption and the fluorescence spectra were measured with a Varian Cary 300 Bio UV/vis spectrometer and Cary eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. To characterize the morphology and size of carbon dots, high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was performed by a MC30 transmission electron microscope (TEM; Philips).

The carbon dots were synthesized by microwave-assisted method. Citric acid (1 g), urea (1 g) and thiourea (1 g) were dissolved in 10 ml distilled water and subsequently was stirred. The solution was irradiated for 5 min in a microwave oven at 450 W. The resulting brown solid was dissolved in 20 ml water and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min and then filtered by a 0.2 µm membrane filter. The resulting clear solution was evaporated until dryness for 3 days at room temperature. Then, solid re-dissolved in 50 ml water.

In a typical assay, a suitable volume of Hg2+ solution and 100 µl of carbon dots (0.298 mg/ml) were added to 3 ml of universal buffer (pH = 9). The fluorescence was recorded at λem = 523 nm and λex = 416 nm in a 1-cm quartz cuvette at room temperature. Slit widths for the excitation and emission were 10 and 10 nm, respectively. The selectivity of the proposed sensor for Hg2+ was evaluated by addition of other metal ions in a similar way.

To prepare of carbon dots/Hg2+ mixture for iodide sensing, 20 µl Hg2+ solution (0.001 M) and 100 µl of carbon dots were added to 3 ml of universal buffer (pH = 9). Then, a suitable volume of I- solution was added to carbon dots/Hg2+ mixture. The fluorescence was recorded at λem = 523 nm and λex = 416 nm in a 1-cm quartz cuvette at room temperature with slit widths of 10 and 10 nm for the excitation and emission, respectively.

The fluorescence quantum yield of N/S co-doped carbon dots was calculated by comparing their integrated fluorescence intensities (excitation at 416 nm) and absorbance values at 416 nm with those of quinine sulfate using the following equation (Lakowicz, 1999):
(1)
ɸ
=
ɸ
R
×
I
I
R
×
A
R
A
×
η
2
η
R
2
where the subscripts R denote standard (quinine sulfate with known fluorescence quantum yield of 0.54) and
ɸ
is the fluorescence quantum yield. I,
η
and A are the measured integrated emission intensity, refractive index of the solvent and optical density, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg2+ and I- in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method was used for determination of Hg2+ and I- concentrations in river, tap and mineral water samples and canned fish samples. The river water samples were filtered through a 0.45 µm membrane filter and then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min. The tap water and mineral water samples did not need to any pretreatment. Different Hg2+ and I- concentrations were spiked and then analyzed with the proposed method.

One gram of canned fish in a 100-ml beaker was mixed with 15 ml of HNO3 and 3 ml HClO4. The mixture was heated for 10 min at 130 °C. Again, 2 ml H2SO4 and HNO3 was added to it. The resulting solution was then heated to reach minimum volume and after cooling at room temperature, transferred into a 25 ml volumetric flask. The pH was adjusted to 9.0 by NaOH solution and diluted to mark by universal buffer (pH = 9). A 0.5 ml portion of the final solution obtained was analyzed for mercury content with the proposed method.

New water-soluble N/S co-doped CDs had three UV–vis absorption peaks at 215, 281and 342 nm ( Fig. 2A). These peaks at 215 and 281 nm were related to electron transitions from π (or n) to π* of C˭C, C˭N, and C˭O (Wang et al., 2010). The fluorescence spectra of the N/S co-doped CDs are shown in Fig. 2A. As shown, when the N/S co-doped CDs were excited at 416 nm, strong fluorescence emission at 523 nm was seen. The maximum fluorescence peak shifted from 523 to 550 nm with change of excitation wavelength from 416 to 496 nm, respectively (Fig. 2B).

The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the N/S co-doped CDs was shown in Fig. 3A and the CDs size was about 10 nm. The surface functional groups of C-dots were characterized by FT-IR study (Fig. 3B). A broad peak at 3433 cm−1 was indicative of OH stretching. Absorption bands at 3329 and 3188 cm−1 were attributed to NH2. The absorption bands at 3050, 1720, 1400–1600 and 1666 cm−1 were characteristics of the aromatic C-H stretching vibrations, C˭O from acid carbocyclic, C-N and stretching vibration band of C˭O indicate the formation of –CONR, respectively. The absorption peaks at 1620, 1404 and 1620 cm−1 were ascribed to the C˭C stretching vibration of aromatic hydrocarbon and symmetric stretching of C˭S. The presence of hydrophilic groups on the surface CDs improved the hydrophilicity and stability of CDs in aqueous system.

Doping of carbon dots with heteroatoms (N, S, P and B) can improve the fluorescence efficiency, selectivity and led to better sensing (Wang et al., 2015). The fluorescence quantum yield of N/S co-doped carbon dots was 19.2% by comparing their integrated fluorescence intensities (excitation at 416 nm) and absorbance values at 416 nm with those of quinine sulfate as a reference.

The effect of pH on the fluorescence of the N/S co-doped CDs in the presence of 20 µM Hg2+ was studied. Relative fluorescence intensity increased by increasing pH of solution and reached to maximum value at pH 9.0 and then decreased ( Fig. 4A). At lower pH, nitrogen and sulfur atoms of CDs were protonated and repulsed Hg2+ ions. Thus, relative fluorescence intensity decreased. Owing to highest fluorescence quenching at pH = 9.0, this pH was chosen for further studies.

The time-dependent fluorescence of the N/S co-doped CDs after addition of Hg (II) was recorded (Fig. 4B). As shown in Fig. 4B, the reaction time of 6 min was enough for the detection of Hg2+ with the N/S co-doped CDs. Fluorescence enhancing was very fast after addition of I-.

Under the optimized conditions (pH = 9.0 and t = 6 min), the fluorescence spectra of the different concentrations of N/S co-doped CDs/Hg2+ system was studied ( Fig. 5A). As shown, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually with increasing of Hg2+ concentration. Fluorescence intensity of N-S-CDs was gradually decreased with increase Hg2+ concentration. Formation of a permanent non-luminescent complex between carbon dots and Hg2+ ions (electron donor and the electron acceptor) can led to quenching of fluorescence (Barman and Sadhukhan, 2012). There was a good linear correlation between F0 /F and the Hg2+concentration in the range of 0.1–20 µM (Fig. 5B). Linear behavior of the relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) could be defined by the Stern-Volmer equation:
(2)
F
0
/ F = K
sv
[ C ]
+ 1
which F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities recorded in absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively, [C] is the concentration of Hg2+ and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. The linear regression equation for Hg2+ was F0/F = 0.0393CHg2+ (µM) + 1.1013 with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.990. The detection limit (LOD) of this method for Hg2+ was 63 nM under the optimal experimental conditions based on LOD = 3 s/m.

Linear range and detection limit of other fluorescence methods for determination of Hg2+ were compared with the proposed sensor ( Table 1). This method for detection of Hg2+ was comparable with tyrosine-based sensor (Kim et al., 2014) in LOD but it had remarkable advantages including wider linear range, simple preparation and its application in aqueous solution. In comparison with graphene quantum dots (Wang et al., 2014), N,S/C-dots (Li et al., 2015) and carbon nitride quantum dots (Cao et al., 2015), new sensor had lower detection limit. Also in comparison with N,S/C-dots sensor, new sensor had excellent selectivity with respect to various metal ions, different amino acids and some organic substance.

In Table 2, the results of the control experiments were summarized. These experiments were carried out by evaluation the N/S co-doped CDs fluorescence spectra after addition of different materials at the same concentration (20 µM). As shown, noticeably interferences were not observed from Na+, K+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Ti3+, Bi3+, Ba2+, Si3+, Mn2+. The fluorescence quenching of carbon dots may be result from strong binding between Hg2+and S atom. The quenching of fluorescence by Hg2+ could be attributed to its ability to facilitate the electron/hole recombination annihilation through an alternate and efficient electron transfer process (Xu et al., 2015). Compared with other metal ions, Hg2+ had a larger ionic radius and polarization, therefore, deformation happened more easily when it interacted with the nitrogen and sulfur atoms (Sun and Lei, 2017). However, some amino acids including glutamic acid, glutamine, cysteine, histidine and I- bring serious interferences for determination of mercury. Because there are not coexisting amino acids with Hg2+ in real sample, the proposed method is useful for these samples.

Under the optimized conditions (pH = 9.0 and time = 1 min), the fluorescence spectra of N/S co-doped carbon dots/Hg2+ system in the presence of different concentrations of I- was also studied ( Fig. 6). As shown, with increasing I- concentration, Hg2+ ions release CDs and fluorescence of CDs was recovered gradually. There was a good linear correlation between F0 /F and I- concentration in the range of 0.1–10 µM, F0/F = 0.0909 I- (µM) + 1.0353 with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.987 (Fig. 6B). The detection limit (LOD) of this method for I- was 74 nM. As shown in Table 1, the proposed I- sensor was comparable with gold nanoclusters (Wang et al., 2012), CdSe quantum dots surface-modified with triethanolamine (Shang et al., 2009), and carbon dots/Hg(II) (Du et al., 2013). New sensor had lower detection limit and good sensitivity and selectivity than previously reported detecting systems. In iodide assay, no interferences were observed from anions such as CN-, SCN-, Br-, F-, S2-, CH3COO- and Cl-.

Real samples such as tap, river, mineral waters and canned fish were analyzed to evaluate the precision and accuracy by this method. The standard addition method was used and results of analysis were summarized in Table 3. Low RSD values indicated excellent precision for Hg2+ and I-. The recovery percentages were from 95.0% to 105.0% for Hg2+ and from 98.45% to 106.56% for iodide.

Water-soluble and new N/S co-doped CDs were synthesized by facile and convenient microwave-assisted method and used as highly sensitive and selective sensor for detection of mercury and iodide in aqueous solution. After addition of Hg2+ to N/S co-doped CDs, the fluorescence of CDs was remarkably quenched. The linear range and limit of detection for Hg2+ were 0.1–20 µM and 63 nM, respectively. With the addition of iodide, Hg2+ ions release CDs and fluoresce of CDs was recovered. The linear range and limit of detection for iodide were 0.1–10 µM and 74 nM, respectively. Thus, this sensor can be used with high sensitivity and selectivity for the determination of Hg2+ and iodide in aqueous solution.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Ilam University Research Council (32/815) for financing the project.
